demographics: I am 21 years old. I am female. I am Black or African American. I have never been married. I completed 11th grade. My family income is $40,000 to $49,999. I am an independent, close to Democrat. I am moderate (middle of the road). My religion is Protestant. I attend religious services about once or twice a year.

events.event_text: Last year, when I was 18, I witnessed a friend being unfairly targeted by the police just because of the color of our skin. It was scary and eye-opening, making me realize how prevalent systemic issues are.

Last year, when I was 20, I met a young man from Brazil who was studying here on an exchange program. We clicked immediately and became best friends. He taught me about his culture and I opened up to him about my experiences with racial profiling, feeling understood in a way I hadn't by others before.

Last year, when I was 20, I was selected to participate in a city-wide dialogue program aimed at bringing together teens from different neighborhoods to discuss our shared experiences and challenges. It was eye-opening to hear the stories of others who, like me, had experienced racial profiling, and we started organizing community events to raise awareness and push for change.
[Step 4 skipped: constraint could not be satisfied — Biological incoherence: active_civic_or_political_participation requires age ≥ 18, got 17]

Last year, at 20, I was stopped by the police while walking home from my shift at the local grocery store. I was confused and scared but luckily, I had my ID and nothing was found. This experience reiterated the racial profiling I've witnessed before, making me determined to continue advocating against this issue in our community.

When I was 17, my best friend since childhood, who was white, invited me to his family vacation at a beach house. It was my first time experiencing such wealth, and it opened my eyes to the privilege that comes with his family's socioeconomic status. I returned home with a newfound awareness of class differences and a determination to fight for equal opportunities for everyone in our community.

At 19, I was accepted into a local internship program with a non-profit focused on Community organizing and Advocacy. It's given me the chance to turn my passion into action, helping our community advocates for change.

At 19, I joined a school walkout to protest gun violence, standing united with students of all races and backgrounds. It was empowering to see our community come together for change, and it strengthened my belief in the power of collective action.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person, at 18, witnessed racial profiling, sparking awareness of systemic issues. At 20, they befriended an exchange student from Brazil, sharing experiences of racial profiling and finding mutual understanding. Also at 20, they participated in a city dialogue program, connecting with others who had experienced racial profiling and organizing community events to advocate for change.

This person, at 20, experienced racial profiling when stopped by police, fueling their advocacy against the issue. At 17, they witnessed wealth and privilege firsthand, sparking a determination to fight for equal opportunities. At 19, they began advocating for change through a non-profit internship and a school walkout protesting gun violence.

persona_card: "Devout but not frequent in religious practice, this 21-year-old Black woman is a moderate Democrat who believes in economic redistribution and a strong government role in ensuring equal opportunities. She holds strong structuralist views on racial inequality, stemming from personal experiences and advocacy, but has low trust in institutions due to witnessing racial profiling and systemic issues. Despite her close-knit community involvement, she maintains an open and welcoming attitude towards social outgroups."
